learning_objectives: Understand the purpose and advantages of database management systems over file-based storage
Identify the components of a DBMS architecture
Differentiate between database models: relational, document, key-value, graph
readings: Codd, 'A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks' (1970)

lecture_notes.introduction: Every application you use is backed by a database. This course teaches you to design, query, and optimize databases — skills required for every software engineering role regardless of specialization.
key_concepts: Data independence: logical vs physical
DBMS architecture: query processor, storage manager, transaction manager
Relational model: tables, rows, columns, keys
ACID properties preview: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability
SQL as a declarative language vs imperative programming
lecture_notes.demonstration: Connect to a PostgreSQL database, explore an existing schema with psql, and run basic SELECT queries to see how relational data is organized

review.value: Physical data independence means the storage format can change without affecting applications; logical data independence means the schema can evolve without breaking existing queries
SQL describes what data you want, not how to get it; the query optimizer decides the execution strategy, freeing programmers from manual data access planning

learning_objectives: Write SQL SELECT queries with WHERE, ORDER BY, and LIMIT
Use aggregate functions and GROUP BY for data summarization
Understand primary keys, foreign keys, and referential integrity

lecture_notes.introduction: SQL is the universal language for relational databases. Mastering SELECT is the foundation; every other operation builds on the ability to precisely describe which rows you need and how to combine them.
key_concepts: SELECT, FROM, WHERE, ORDER BY, LIMIT
Aggregate functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX
GROUP BY and HAVING for grouped aggregation
NULL handling: IS NULL, COALESCE, three-valued logic
Primary keys, foreign keys, and ON DELETE/UPDATE actions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Write progressively complex queries against the university database: enrollment counts per department, average GPA by major, students with no enrolled courses

review.value: NULL represents unknown, not zero or empty string; any comparison with NULL yields NULL (not true or false), which is why you must use IS NULL instead of = NULL
Foreign keys ensure that every reference to another table points to an existing row; the database rejects inserts/updates that would create orphaned references

learning_objectives: Write INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, and FULL OUTER JOIN queries
Apply UNION, INTERSECT, and EXCEPT for set operations

lecture_notes.introduction: Real queries span multiple tables. Joins are the mechanism for combining related data from different tables, and understanding which join type to use is essential for getting correct results.
key_concepts: INNER JOIN: matching rows from both tables
LEFT/RIGHT OUTER JOIN: preserving all rows from one side
FULL OUTER JOIN: preserving all rows from both sides
Subqueries in WHERE, FROM, and SELECT clauses
Correlated subqueries: referencing outer query values
UNION, INTERSECT, EXCEPT for combining result sets
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a report showing all students, their enrolled courses (if any), and their grades, using LEFT JOIN to include students with no enrollments
setup_requirements: Familiarity with basic SELECT from previous session

review.value: INNER JOIN returns only rows where both tables have matching keys; LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table plus matching rows from the right, filling NULLs where no match exists
A subquery that references a column from the outer query, causing it to be re-evaluated for each outer row; often can be rewritten as a JOIN for better performance

learning_objectives: Design database schemas using entity-relationship diagrams
Apply normalization rules through third normal form (3NF)
Identify and eliminate update, insertion, and deletion anomalies

lecture_notes.introduction: A poorly designed database causes data anomalies, wasted storage, and complex queries. Normalization is a systematic process for organizing tables to eliminate redundancy and ensure data integrity.
key_concepts: Entity-relationship diagrams: entities, attributes, relationships
Cardinality: one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many
Functional dependencies and candidate keys
1NF: atomic values, no repeating groups
2NF: no partial dependencies on composite keys
3NF: no transitive dependencies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Take a denormalized spreadsheet of order data with repeated customer and product information, identify all anomalies, and normalize it step by step to 3NF

review.value: When the same fact is stored in multiple rows, updating one copy but not the others creates inconsistency; normalization ensures each fact is stored exactly once

A table is in 3NF if for every non-trivial functional dependency X -> A, either X is a superkey or A is part of a candidate key; informally, every non-key attribute depends on the key, the whole key, and nothing but the key

learning_objectives: Understand B-tree and hash index structures
Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to diagnose query performance
Create indexes to optimize slow queries
readings: PostgreSQL Documentation: EXPLAIN and Index Types

lecture_notes.introduction: A query that scans every row in a million-row table takes seconds; the same query with a proper index takes milliseconds. Understanding indexing is the difference between an application that works and one that scales.
key_concepts: B-tree indexes: balanced tree structure, O(log n) lookups
Hash indexes: O(1) equality lookups, no range support
Composite indexes and column ordering
EXPLAIN ANALYZE: reading query execution plans
Index trade-offs: faster reads vs slower writes and storage cost
lecture_notes.demonstration: Run a slow query on a 500,000-row table, use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to identify a sequential scan, create the appropriate index, and re-run to observe the speedup
setup_requirements: PostgreSQL with large sample dataset loaded
psql or pgAdmin for EXPLAIN ANALYZE

review.value: B-trees keep all leaf nodes at the same depth, ensuring O(log n) operations; internal nodes have high fanout (hundreds of children) minimizing disk I/O, making them ideal for database indexes
An index on (A, B) supports queries filtering on A alone or on both A and B, but not on B alone; column order in composite indexes must match query filter patterns

learning_objectives: Understand ACID properties and transaction isolation levels
Identify concurrency anomalies: dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, phantom reads
Explain write-ahead logging and crash recovery basics
readings: Silberschatz, Ch. 14-15 - Transactions and Concurrency Control

lecture_notes.introduction: Databases serve many users simultaneously. Transactions ensure that concurrent operations do not corrupt data, and recovery mechanisms ensure data survives crashes. These guarantees are what distinguish a database from a file.
key_concepts: ACID: Atomicity (all or nothing), Consistency (valid states), Isolation (no interference), Durability (survives crashes)
Isolation levels: READ UNCOMMITTED, READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, SERIALIZABLE
Concurrency anomalies and which isolation levels prevent each
Locking: shared vs exclusive locks, deadlock detection
Write-ahead logging (WAL): ensuring durability and enabling recovery
lecture_notes.demonstration: Open two psql sessions, demonstrate a dirty read at READ UNCOMMITTED, then show how REPEATABLE READ prevents it, and trigger a deadlock to observe PostgreSQL's automatic detection
setup_requirements: Two terminal windows with psql sessions

review.value: Higher isolation prevents more anomalies but reduces concurrency (more blocking); READ COMMITTED is the PostgreSQL default and sufficient for most applications; SERIALIZABLE is safest but slowest

Before modifying any data page, the change is recorded in a sequential log on disk; on crash, the log is replayed to bring the database to a consistent state, ensuring durability without requiring every write to immediately flush to data files

questions.question: Write a SQL query that returns the names of all students who are enrolled in more than 3 courses, along with their enrollment count. Use the tables: students(id, name) and enrollments(student_id, course_id).
questions.answer:
SELECT s.name, COUNT(e.course_id) AS course_count
FROM students s
JOIN enrollments e ON s.id = e.student_id
GROUP BY s.id, s.name
HAVING COUNT(e.course_id) > 3;

rubric.key: Correct JOIN between students and enrollments

questions.question: Explain the difference between WHERE and HAVING in a SQL query. When must you use HAVING instead of WHERE?
questions.answer: WHERE filters individual rows before grouping occurs. HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY and aggregation. You must use HAVING when the filter condition involves an aggregate function (COUNT, SUM, AVG, etc.) because aggregate results do not exist until after grouping.

rubric.key: Correctly explains WHERE filters before grouping
Correctly explains HAVING filters after grouping
Explains that aggregate functions require HAVING

questions.question: Given a table Orders(order_id, customer_name, customer_email, product_name, product_price, quantity), identify all functional dependencies, determine the highest normal form, and normalize to 3NF.
questions.answer:
Functional dependencies: order_id -> customer_name, customer_email, product_name, product_price, quantity; customer_name -> customer_email (assuming unique names); product_name -> product_price.

The table is in 1NF but not 2NF (assuming composite key scenarios) or 3NF due to transitive dependencies (order_id -> customer_name -> customer_email, order_id -> product_name -> product_price).

3NF decomposition:
- Customers(customer_name, customer_email)
- Products(product_name, product_price)
- Orders(order_id, customer_name, product_name, quantity)

With foreign keys from Orders to Customers and Products.

questions.question: A query SELECT * FROM orders WHERE customer_id = 42 AND order_date > '2024-01-01' ORDER BY order_date is slow on a table with 2 million rows. What index would you create and why?
questions.answer:
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_cust_date ON orders(customer_id, order_date);

The composite index should have customer_id first because it is an equality condition, and order_date second because it is used for both a range filter and ORDER BY. This allows the index to narrow to the specific customer, then scan dates in order, avoiding a separate sort step.

rubric.key: Correct composite index with proper column order
Explains why customer_id comes first (equality before range)
Notes the index also satisfies ORDER BY

major_assignments.title: Database Design and Implementation Project
major_assignments.description: Design and implement a complete database for an e-commerce application. Start from requirements, produce an ER diagram, normalize to 3NF, implement in PostgreSQL with proper constraints and indexes, populate with realistic data, and write a suite of complex queries including reports and analytics.
objectives: Apply the full database design process from requirements to implementation
Produce a normalized schema with proper constraints and referential integrity
Write complex SQL queries for business reporting and analytics
Optimize query performance using indexes and EXPLAIN ANALYZE
deliverables: ER diagram with all entities, attributes, and relationships
SQL DDL script creating all tables with constraints and indexes
Data population script with at least 1000 orders across 100 customers
Ten complex queries with EXPLAIN ANALYZE output and optimization notes
Design document (3-4 pages) explaining schema decisions and normalization

criteria: ER diagram accurately models the domain with correct cardinalities
Schema is in 3NF with no redundancy
All constraints (PK, FK, NOT NULL, CHECK, UNIQUE) are appropriate
Data types are chosen correctly for each column
All ten queries produce correct results
Queries demonstrate JOIN, subquery, aggregation, and window function usage
EXPLAIN ANALYZE output is provided and interpreted correctly
At least two queries are optimized with indexes and performance is documented
Population script generates realistic, varied data
Referential integrity is maintained across all data
Sufficient volume to demonstrate performance characteristics
No dummy or placeholder values
Design document explains all schema decisions with rationale
Normalization steps are shown explicitly
Index choices are justified with query patterns
Professional writing and formatting
Well-designed 3NF schema with comprehensive constraints
Complex SQL queries demonstrating advanced SQL proficiency
Performance analysis with EXPLAIN ANALYZE and index optimization
Thorough documentation of design decisions and normalization

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of SQL and database concepts

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on SQL and database design exercises

major_assignments.description: Comprehensive database design and implementation project

midterm_examination.description: Written and SQL exam covering relational model and query writing

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam including optimization and transactions

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures and mandatory for lab sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences may result in a reduced participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of SQL approaches is encouraged. All queries, schemas, and written analysis must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Copying SQL queries from the internet or other students without attribution constitutes plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need PostgreSQL 16+, a database client (pgAdmin or DBeaver), and access to the course database server. Lab computers are available.
